mb.title: Message Broker Visualizer
rabbitmq.name: RabbitMQ
rabbitmq.modes.hello.label: 1. Direct Queue
rabbitmq.modes.hello.desc: P → Queue → C. Producer writes to a named queue, Consumer reads from it. One to one.
rabbitmq.modes.work.label: 2. Round-Robin
rabbitmq.modes.work.desc: Queue distributes tasks among workers via round-robin. Each message is processed by exactly one worker. Used for parallel processing of heavy tasks.
rabbitmq.modes.pubsub.label: 3. Pub/Sub
rabbitmq.modes.pubsub.desc: Fanout Exchange broadcasts every message to all subscribers simultaneously. Used for logging, cache invalidation, realtime updates.
rabbitmq.modes.routing.label: 4. Routing
rabbitmq.modes.routing.desc: Direct Exchange delivers messages only to queues with exact binding key match. Routing key is set by the producer.
rabbitmq.modes.topics.label: 5. Topics
rabbitmq.modes.topics.desc: Topic Exchange matches routing key by patterns: * — exactly one word, # — any number of words. Flexible event filtering by hierarchy.
rabbitmq.modes.rpc.label: 6. RPC
rabbitmq.modes.rpc.desc: RPC over queue: client sends a request with correlation_id and reply_to queue. Server processes and replies. Client waits for response in its temporary queue.
rabbitmq.modes.confirms.label: 7. Confirms
rabbitmq.modes.confirms.desc: Publisher Confirms — broker acknowledges each message write via ACK/NACK. Guarantees at-least-once delivery on the producer side.
rabbitmq.details.hello.principles: Simplest messaging pattern: one producer sends directly to a named queue, one consumer reads from it
Messages are stored in the queue until a consumer is ready to process them (asynchronous decoupling)
Default exchange ("") routes messages to queues by name — no explicit exchange binding needed
Messages are removed from the queue once acknowledged by the consumer (at-most-once without ack)
Queue must be declared before use; both producer and consumer can declare it (idempotent operation)

rabbitmq.details.hello.concepts.definition: FIFO buffer that stores messages until they are consumed. Durable queues survive broker restarts.
Nameless exchange ("") that routes messages to the queue whose name matches the routing key.
Consumer sends ACK after processing. Without ACK, messages are re-delivered if consumer disconnects.
Queue that persists across broker restarts. Combined with persistent messages for full durability.
TCP connection to the broker. Channels are lightweight virtual connections multiplexed over one TCP connection.

rabbitmq.details.work.principles: Work queue distributes tasks among multiple workers via round-robin dispatching
Each message is delivered to exactly one worker — competing consumers pattern
prefetch_count=1 ensures a worker receives the next message only after acknowledging the current one
Fair dispatch prevents fast workers from being idle while slow workers are overloaded
Manual acknowledgment ensures messages are requeued if a worker crashes mid-processing

rabbitmq.details.work.concepts.definition: Default dispatch: broker sends messages to consumers in rotation. Does not consider consumer load.
QoS setting (prefetch_count) limits how many unacknowledged messages a consumer can hold. Key to fair dispatch.
Multiple consumers on the same queue. Each message goes to exactly one consumer. Enables horizontal scaling.
Time-to-live for messages. Expired messages are dead-lettered or discarded. Set per-queue or per-message.
Messages that are rejected, expire, or exceed queue length are routed to a dead-letter exchange for inspection.

rabbitmq.details.pubsub.principles: Fanout exchange broadcasts every message to all bound queues — true publish/subscribe pattern
Each subscriber gets its own queue; messages are copied to every bound queue independently
Producers don't know which queues exist — full decoupling between publishers and subscribers
Temporary (exclusive, auto-delete) queues are typical for subscribers that don't need persistence
Adding a new subscriber requires no changes to the producer — just bind a new queue to the exchange

rabbitmq.details.pubsub.concepts.definition: Ignores routing keys entirely. Delivers a copy of every message to every bound queue. One-to-many pattern.
Link between an exchange and a queue. For fanout exchanges, the binding key is ignored.
Queue that only one connection can use. Automatically deleted when the connection closes. Ideal for temporary subscribers.
Receives messages from producers and routes them to queues based on type (fanout, direct, topic, headers).
Queue or exchange that is automatically deleted when the last consumer disconnects.

rabbitmq.details.routing.principles: Direct exchange routes messages only to queues whose binding key exactly matches the routing key
Multiple queues can bind to the same exchange with the same routing key — messages are copied to all of them
A queue can have multiple bindings with different routing keys to receive messages from several sources
Routing key is set by the producer at publish time; binding key is set by the consumer at bind time
Direct exchange enables selective message filtering — consumers receive only the message types they need

rabbitmq.details.routing.concepts.definition: Routes messages by exact routing key match. Message goes only to queues bound with the same key.
String set by the producer on each message. Direct exchange compares it to binding keys for routing decisions.
String specified when binding a queue to an exchange. Must match the routing key exactly for direct exchanges.
Each consumer binds with specific keys and only receives matching messages. Unlike fanout, not all messages go everywhere.
A queue can bind multiple times with different keys to the same exchange, receiving messages matching any of them.

rabbitmq.details.topics.principles: Topic exchange matches routing keys by dot-separated word patterns using * (one word) and # (zero or more words)
Routing key must be a dot-separated string (e.g., "order.created.eu") — max 255 bytes
Pattern * substitutes exactly one word; # substitutes zero or more words — powerful filtering hierarchy
A topic exchange with "#" binding behaves like fanout; with no wildcards, it behaves like direct
Topic exchanges enable event hierarchies: "order.*" gets all order events, "*.created.*" gets all creation events

rabbitmq.details.topics.concepts.definition: Routes messages by pattern matching on dot-separated routing keys. Supports * and # wildcards.
Matches exactly one word in the routing key. "order.*.eu" matches "order.created.eu" but not "order.eu".
Matches zero or more words. "order.#" matches "order", "order.created", "order.created.eu", etc.
A segment between dots in the routing key. "stock.usd.nyse" has three words: "stock", "usd", "nyse".
Topic keys form a natural hierarchy (domain.event.region) enabling flexible subscription at any level.

rabbitmq.details.rpc.principles: RPC over messaging: client sends request with reply_to queue and correlation_id, server processes and replies
Each client creates an exclusive callback queue to receive responses — one queue per client connection
correlation_id matches responses to requests, enabling multiple outstanding RPCs on the same callback queue
Server is stateless — it simply consumes from the request queue and publishes to the reply_to address
RPC over messaging adds reliability (retries, persistence) but introduces latency vs direct HTTP calls

rabbitmq.details.rpc.concepts.definition: Unique identifier set on the request message. The server copies it to the response so the client can match request-response pairs.
Exclusive queue created by the client. Its name is sent in the reply_to property so the server knows where to send the response.
Same as reply-to queue. Client listens on this queue for RPC responses. Typically exclusive and auto-delete.
Pattern where one party sends a request and waits for a response. Over messaging, this is asynchronous but feels synchronous to the caller.
Client should set a timeout for RPC responses. If the server crashes, the client must handle the missing response gracefully.

rabbitmq.details.confirms.principles: Publisher confirms: broker acknowledges (ACK/NACK) each message after it is written to disk or delivered
Without confirms, the producer has no way to know if the broker received and persisted the message
Messages are confirmed in order; a single ACK with multiple=true can confirm all messages up to that sequence number
NACK indicates the broker could not handle the message (e.g., disk full, internal error)
Publisher confirms + consumer ACK + durable queues + persistent messages = end-to-end at-least-once delivery

rabbitmq.details.confirms.concepts.definition: Broker sends ACK back to the producer after successfully queuing/persisting a message. Async confirmation.
Monotonically increasing number assigned to each published message. Used to track which messages are confirmed.
ACK with multiple=true confirms all messages up to the given sequence number. More efficient than per-message confirms.
Negative acknowledgment from broker. Indicates the message was not processed. Producer should retry or handle the failure.
Delivery guarantee: message is delivered at least once (may be duplicated). Achieved with confirms + persistent messages + durable queues.

kafka.name: Apache Kafka
kafka.modes.partitions.label: Partitions
kafka.modes.partitions.desc: Kafka distributes messages across partitions. Strategies: Round-Robin (no key), Key-Based (hash), or Manual. Messages within a partition are strictly ordered.
kafka.modes.consumer-groups.label: Consumer Groups
kafka.modes.consumer-groups.desc: Each partition is consumed by exactly one consumer in a group. Groups are independent. Offset is tracked per-group.
kafka.modes.retention.label: Retention & Replay
kafka.modes.retention.desc: Kafka stores messages in an ordered log. Consumer group reads independently and can seek offset back for replay. Messages are not deleted after reading.
kafka.modes.exactly-once.label: Exactly-Once
kafka.modes.exactly-once.desc: Delivery guarantees: at-most-once (acks=0, may lose), at-least-once (acks=1, may duplicate), exactly-once (idempotent producer, no loss or dups).
kafka.details.partitions.principles: A Kafka topic is split into partitions — each partition is an ordered, immutable log of messages
Messages within a single partition are strictly ordered; no global ordering guarantee across partitions
Partition assignment strategies: Round-Robin (no key), Key-Based (hash of key mod partition count), Manual
Partitions are the unit of parallelism — more partitions allow more consumers to read in parallel
Each partition is replicated across brokers (replication factor) for fault tolerance

kafka.details.partitions.concepts.definition: An ordered, append-only log within a topic. Each message gets a sequential offset. Partitions enable parallelism.
Sequential position of a message within a partition. Consumers track their position by offset.
Messages with the same key always go to the same partition (hash(key) mod N). Guarantees per-key ordering.
Each partition has one leader (handles reads/writes) and N-1 followers (replicate data). Leader election on failure.
In-Sync Replicas — followers that are caught up with the leader. Only ISR members can become the new leader.

kafka.details.consumer-groups.principles: Each partition is consumed by exactly one consumer within a consumer group — no duplicate processing
Different consumer groups are independent — each group processes the full stream independently
When a consumer joins or leaves, partitions are rebalanced across remaining group members
If there are more consumers than partitions in a group, excess consumers sit idle
Consumer group offset tracking enables each group to read at its own pace

kafka.details.consumer-groups.concepts.definition: Named group of consumers that collectively read a topic. Each partition is assigned to exactly one member.
Partition reassignment triggered when a consumer joins, leaves, or crashes. Causes brief processing pause.
Broker responsible for managing consumer group membership and partition assignment.
Last processed offset per partition, stored in __consumer_offsets topic. Enables resume after restart.
Difference between the latest message offset and the consumer's committed offset. High lag = consumer falling behind.

kafka.details.retention.principles: Kafka retains messages based on time (retention.ms) or size (retention.bytes), not consumption status
Messages are NOT deleted after consumption — multiple consumer groups can re-read the same data
Log compaction keeps only the latest value per key, enabling changelog/snapshot patterns
Consumers can seek to any offset (beginning, end, timestamp, specific offset) for replay or reprocessing
Retention policy is set per topic — different topics can have different retention periods

kafka.details.retention.concepts.definition: How long messages are kept. retention.ms=604800000 (7 days) is default. -1 means keep forever.
Instead of time-based deletion, keeps only the latest value per key. Like a changelog with deduplication.
Partition is split into segment files. Retention and compaction operate on whole segments, not individual messages.
What happens when a consumer has no committed offset: auto.offset.reset=earliest (start from beginning) or latest (only new messages).
Consumers can seek to an earlier offset to reprocess messages. Enables fixing bugs and rebuilding state.

kafka.details.exactly-once.principles: Three delivery guarantees: at-most-once (may lose), at-least-once (may duplicate), exactly-once (no loss, no dups)
Idempotent producer assigns sequence numbers per partition — broker deduplicates retried messages
Transactions enable atomic writes across multiple partitions and topics (all-or-nothing)
acks=all ensures the leader waits for all ISR replicas to confirm before acknowledging the produce request
Exactly-once semantics (EOS) combines idempotent producer + transactions + consumer read_committed isolation

kafka.details.exactly-once.concepts.definition: Producer assigns sequence numbers. Broker detects and discards duplicate messages from retries. enable.idempotence=true.
Unique ID assigned to each producer by the broker. Combined with sequence number for deduplication.
Atomic write across partitions. All messages in a transaction are either all committed or all aborted.
Producer waits for all in-sync replicas to acknowledge. Strongest durability guarantee but higher latency.
Consumer isolation level that only reads committed (non-transactional or committed transactional) messages.

redis.name: Redis
redis.modes.pubsub.label: Pub/Sub
redis.modes.pubsub.desc: Redis Pub/Sub — fire-and-forget. Messages are not stored. If a subscriber is disconnected at the time of sending, it will not receive the message. Suitable for realtime events without guarantees.
redis.modes.pattern.label: Pattern Sub
redis.modes.pattern.desc: Redis PSUBSCRIBE matches channels by glob patterns: * (any chars), ? (one char), [abc] (one of). Allows subscribing to multiple channels at once.
redis.modes.streams.label: Streams
redis.modes.streams.desc: Redis Streams — persistent event log with consumer groups. Unlike Pub/Sub, messages are stored and available for re-reading. Supports XACK for at-least-once guarantees.
redis.modes.eventsourcing.label: Event Sourcing
redis.modes.eventsourcing.desc: Event Sourcing: system state is not stored directly — it is reconstructed by replaying events. Redis Stream stores an immutable log. Rebuild replays the full history.
redis.details.pubsub.principles: Redis Pub/Sub is fire-and-forget: messages are NOT stored and NOT persisted to disk
If a subscriber is disconnected at the moment of publishing, it will never receive that message
There is no acknowledgment mechanism — the publisher does not know if anyone received the message
Messages are delivered to all currently connected subscribers on the channel simultaneously
Pub/Sub operates outside the key space — SUBSCRIBE/PUBLISH do not interact with normal Redis keys

redis.details.pubsub.concepts.definition: Named message bus. Publishers send to a channel, subscribers listen on it. No persistence, no history.
Blocks the connection and listens for messages on one or more channels. Connection becomes subscribe-only.
Sends a message to a channel. Returns the number of subscribers that received it (0 if none).
Messages are immediately dispatched to current subscribers and then discarded. No retry, no storage.
A subscribed client enters a special mode — it can only run SUBSCRIBE, UNSUBSCRIBE, PSUBSCRIBE, PUNSUBSCRIBE, PING, RESET.

redis.details.pattern.principles: PSUBSCRIBE matches channels using glob-style patterns: * (any chars), ? (one char), [abc] (character class)
A single pattern subscription can match multiple channels simultaneously without knowing their exact names
Pattern matching is evaluated on every PUBLISH — high number of patterns can increase CPU load
A client can mix regular SUBSCRIBE and PSUBSCRIBE — it receives messages from both
If both SUBSCRIBE and PSUBSCRIBE match the same channel, the client receives the message twice

redis.details.pattern.concepts.definition: Subscribe using a glob pattern. "chat:*" matches chat:room1, chat:room2, etc. Checked on every publish.
Matches any sequence of characters. "events:*" matches "events:created", "events:deleted", "events:foo:bar".
Matches exactly one character. "log?" matches "log1", "logA", but not "log" or "log12".
PUBSUB NUMPAT returns the number of active pattern subscriptions. Many patterns = higher per-publish CPU cost.
If a client subscribes to both "chat:room1" (exact) and "chat:*" (pattern), it gets the same message twice.

redis.details.streams.principles: Redis Streams provide a persistent, append-only log with consumer groups — unlike ephemeral Pub/Sub
Each entry has a unique auto-generated ID (timestamp-sequence) and one or more field-value pairs
Consumer groups track per-consumer progress; messages are not re-delivered to other group members
XACK explicitly acknowledges processing — unacknowledged messages remain in the Pending Entries List (PEL)
XCLAIM allows transferring ownership of pending messages from a failed consumer to another

redis.details.streams.concepts.definition: Append-only log data structure. Each entry has ID + field-value pairs. Persisted to disk like any Redis key.
Format: timestamp-sequence (e.g., 1234567890-0). Auto-generated, monotonically increasing. Used for range queries.
Named group that reads from a stream. Each entry is delivered to one consumer in the group. Tracks last-delivered-id.
Pending Entries List — entries delivered to a consumer but not yet XACK'd. Enables at-least-once delivery guarantee.
Transfers ownership of a pending entry from one consumer to another. Used to handle consumer failures.

redis.details.eventsourcing.principles: Event Sourcing: application state is derived by replaying an immutable sequence of events, not stored directly
Redis Stream serves as the event store — append-only, ordered, persistent, with consumer group support
State is rebuilt by reading all events from the stream and applying each event's transformation in order
Snapshots can be periodically saved to avoid replaying the entire history for state reconstruction
Events are immutable — to correct a mistake, append a compensating event rather than modifying history

redis.details.eventsourcing.concepts.definition: Persistent log of domain events in order. Redis Stream provides this with XADD (write) and XRANGE (read).
Process of replaying all events from the beginning to reconstruct the current application state.
A read-optimized view derived from the event stream. Can be rebuilt at any time by replaying events.
An event that reverses the effect of a previous event. Events are never deleted or modified — only appended.
Periodic capture of current state to avoid replaying the full event history. State = snapshot + events after snapshot.
